coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive ratiometric aptasensor based on the dual-potential electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in a novel ternary system for detection of Patulin in fruit products
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Liangrui
Chen, QingQing
Jing, Chunyang
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: To sensitively monitor trace-level of toxic patulin (PAT), an ultrasensitive PAT ratiometric aptasensor based on the dual-potential electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was first proposed. Noteworthily, Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped trimetallic nanocube (Ru@Tri) innovatively integrated the luminophore and cathode coreaction accelerator (CCA), which could generate strong cathodic ECL in the existence of low concentration of K2S2O8. Simultaneously, anthocyanin-derived carbon quantum dots (anth-CQDs) prepared from purple potato skins was first served as a green anodic coreactant. And SiO2-coated anth-CQDs (anth-CQDs@SiO2) exhibited excellent performance for enhancing anodic ECL of Ru@Tri. Based on this, a novel ternary ECL system was established. In the presence of PAT, the ECL intensity ratio of anode to cathode (I ECL-A/I ECL-C) was significantly increased, and a low detection limit of 0.05 pg mL−1 was obtained. Moreover, when proposed method and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) were simultaneously applied to series of fruit products, the obtained results were completely consistent, reflecting its practicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor
Ratiometric
Patulin
Ru(bpy)3 2+
Ternary-system

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction and detection procedure of dual-potential ECL aptasensor
Possible reaction mechanism of the ratiometric ECL aptaensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image, (B) partial enlargement SEM image and (C) EDS spectrum of Ru@Tri, XPS high resolution spectrum of (D) Ru, (E) Ag, (F) Au, (G) Pt, (H) TEM image and (I) XRD patterns of anth-CQDs, (J) TEM image of anth-CQDs@SiO2, (K) FTIR spectrum of anth-CQDs and anth-CQDs@SiO2.

(A) Agarose gel electrophoresis images of nucleic acids in HCR products: 2 μM cDNA (a), 1 μM cDNA + 1 μM aptamer (b), 1 μM cDNA + 1 μM HP1 + 1 μM HP2 (c),1 μM cDNA + 1 μM aptamer + 1 μM HP1 + 1 μM HP2 (d), 1 μM cDNA + 1 μM aptamer + 1 μM HP1 + 1 μM HP2 + 1 ng mL−1 PAT (e), (B) EIS and (C) CV curves of bare GE (a), PDDA/GE (b), Ru@Tri/GE (c), cDNA/Ru@Tri/GE (d), BSA/Ru@Tri/GE (e), dsDNA/Ru@Tri/GE (f), PAT/Ru@Tri/GE (g) and HCRcomplex/Ru@Tri/GE (h), and (D) CV and ECL profile of the constructed dual-potential ECL aptasensor before (a) and after (b) incubated with 1 pg mL−1 PAT in PBSECL.

(A) ECL-potential curves of AuPtAg/GE (a), Ru@AuAg/GE (b), Ru@PtAg/GE (c), and Ru@Tri/GE (d) in PBSECL. Inset is the anodic ECL in a larger version. (B) UV–vis absorption and (C) CV curves of 2 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+ (a), 0.2 mg mL−1 anth-CQDs (b), and anth-CQDs/Ru(bpy)3 2+ (c) in PBS. (D) ECL-potential curves of Ru@Tri/GE (a), anth-CQDs/Ru@Tri/GE (b), and anth-CQDs@SiO2/Ru@Tri/GE (c) in PBSECL, and Ru@Tri/GE (d) in PBS containing 5 mM TPrA. (E) I ECL-A/I ECL-C of Ru@Tri/GE in untreated, O2 saturated, and N2 saturated PBSECL containing 0.2 mg mL−1 anth-CQDs@SiO2. (F) ECL mechanism of the ratiometric ECL aptasensor. PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4).

(A) ECL-time curves of the aptasensors with 0.1 pg mL−1 to 10 ng mL−1 PAT, (B) Logarithmic calibration curve between I ECL-A/I ECL-C and PAT concentrations. Repeatability analysis of the ratiometric ECL aptasensor with 1 ng mL−1 PAT based on (C) I ECL-A and I ECL-C, respectively and (D) I ECL-A/I ECL-C. (E) Continuous cyclic scans and long stability of the aptasensor with 1 pg mL−1 PAT, and (F) Selectivity of the proposed ECL aptasensor with 0.1 ng mL−1 PAT compared to possible interfering mycotoxin: 1 ng mL−1 AFB1, OTA, ZEN and their mixture.
Schematic diagram of the ratiometric ECL aptasensor.
Results of the determination of PAT in fruit product samples obtained from the proposed ECL aptasensor and HPLC (n = 5).

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive ratiometric aptasensor based on the dual-potential electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in a novel ternary system for detection of Patulin in fruit products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To sensitively monitor trace-level of toxic patulin (PAT), an ultrasensitive PAT ratiometric aptasensor based on the dual-potential electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was first proposed. Noteworthily, Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped trimetallic nanocube (Ru@Tri) innovatively integrated the luminophore and cathode coreaction accelerator (CCA), which could generate strong cathodic ECL in the existence of low concentration of K2S2O8. Simultaneously, anthocyanin-derived carbon quantum dots (anth-CQDs) prepared from purple potato skins was first served as a green anodic coreactant. And SiO2-coated anth-CQDs (anth-CQDs@SiO2) exhibited excellent performance for enhancing anodic ECL of Ru@Tri. Based on this, a novel ternary ECL system was established. In the presence of PAT, the ECL intensity ratio of anode to cathode (I ECL-A/I ECL-C) was significantly increased, and a low detection limit of 0.05 pg mL−1 was obtained. Moreover, when proposed method and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) were simultaneously applied to series of fruit products, the obtained results were completely consistent, reflecting its practicability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins are secondary metabolites produced by various fungus at the right temperature and humidity, which are obstinate during food processing (Hou et al., 2023). Due to their teratogenic, carcinogenic and mutagenic properties, dietary exposure to mycotoxins is a major public health problem and has attracted worldwide attention (Agriopoulou, Stamatelopoulou & Varzakas, 2020). Standard mycotoxin detection methods are usually performed in certified laboratories using sophisticated instruments such as high performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence (HPLC-FL) or high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Keskina & Eyupoglu, 2023). Although these methods can provide accurate detection results, they are expensive, time-consuming and require specialist technicians. To improve the timeliness of mycotoxin regulation, researchers paid more attention to biosensors with fast analysis speed and miniaturized instruments. Attractively, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor has the merits of simple operation, excellent sensitivity, and wide measuring range (Jia et al., 2022). With the continuous development, the ratiometric ECL biosensor (ECLR-biosensor) for mycotoxins was established, where an internal calibration mechanism is introduced to further improve the accuracy, reproducibility, and sensitivity (Lv et al., 2022). However, rigorous resign that concentrated on eligible luminophore pairs based on competing for the same coreactant or ECL resonance energy transferor (ECL-RET) was required. The coexistence of multiple luminophores and coreactants may interfere with the target, and ECL-RET requires the wavelength overlap of the ECL energy donor and acceptor, which confined its further practical applications and development in mycotoxin analysis.

A breakthrough can be obtained via the choice of single-luminophore that can achieve dual-potential ECL emission. With the feature of low metal–ligand charge transfer excited states, high quantum yield, and good electrochemical reversibility, tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and its derivatives are the most classic ECL luminophore been widely used in ECL biosensors (Sobhanie et al., 2022). In the presence of suitable coreactant, it can achieve dual-potential ECL at the anode and cathode simultaneously. Thereby, the fussy design for double emitters can be avoided. However, as far as we all know, there are only three related reports and those never been applied for mycotoxin detection. For instance, gold nanoparticles groomed carbon nitride nanosheets were proven could be cathode coreactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+. Combined with the enhancement effect of Hg2+ to anodic ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+, an ECLR-biosensor was established for Hg2+ detection (Cao et al., 2020). Ju’ group added tripropylamine (TEA) directly to Ru(bpy)3 2+ functionalized metal–organic framework to modify the electrode, which possessed dual-potential ECL in the existence of coreactant K2S2O8, and was employed for testing neuron-specific enolase (Dong et al., 2021). Moreover, an ECLR-biosensor was designed to detect mucin1 protein based on the dual-potential ECL of nanostructures of Ru(bpy)3 2+ modified electrode, in which black phosphorus quantum dot was used as an anodic nano-coreactant and K2S2O8 was cathodic coreactant (Yin et al., 2021). All the above reports are based on the binary ECL system (luminophore/coreactants), where ECL intensity can only be improved by increasing the consumption of Ru(bpy)3 2+ or coreactant. Given the ECL properties and current research reports, the following key points need to be concerned to further establish the more workable ECLR-biosensor using Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives as a single-luminophore. First, due to the excellent water solubility, effective immobilization of Ru(bpy)3 2+ on the electrode is vital and uneasy. Current methods have shortcomings such as complicated steps and large testing agent consumption. Second, tripropylamine (TPrA) and TEA are typical anodic coreactant, and S2O8 2− is a typical cathodic coreactant. However, TPrA and TEA are toxic and volatile, which may produce potential hazards to the environment and human health. The strong cathodic ECL requires high concentrations of S2O8 2− (80 ∼ 100 mM), whose strong oxidation inevitably causes damage to the biosensing interface. The development of new nano-alternatives and the reduction of dosage are attractive. Third, more sensitive target response approaches need to be adopted, and current studies are based only on the sandwich or competitive substitution mode.

Intending to overcome the above obstacles, the author believes some innovation and exploration should be carried out. To begin with, precious metal nanomaterials with excellent conductivity, large specific surface area, and good biocompatibility are the ideal carriers for Ru(bpy)3 2+. Recently, researchers reported yet another fascinating property of precious metal nanomaterials, which could catalyze the conversion of S2O8 2− to SO4 .− (Lei et al., 2018). Thus it can be introduced into the binary system as cathode coreaction accelerator (CCA) to form a ternary (luminophore/coreactant/CCA) ECL system and to improve cathode ECL of luminophor. Therein, we first report that Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped trimetallic nanocube (Ru@Tri) synthesized by facile low-temperature method could play the dual role of luminophore and CCA. And the synergistic catalytic effect of multiple noble metals provides a guarantee for realizing strong cathode ECL in low concentrations of S2O8 2− synchronously. In addition, several studies have been reported that amino passivated carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesized from chemicals can improve the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+ as an anodic nano-coreactant (Luo et al., 2019). And it seems to be an ideal alternative to TPrA due to good biocompatibility, water solubility and low toxicity. With the deepening of the environmental protection concept, biowaste as a sustainable, green and cost-effective carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs has been reported (Cao et al., 2020). Unfortunately, there is no attempt and research by utilizing CQDs synthesized from biowaste to act as nano-coreactant for anodic ECL. In this work, we collect abandoned purple potato peel to extract anthocyanin stock and innovatively employed the stock as a carbon source to prepare harmless CQDs (anth-CQDs) without further passivation as a green anodic coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+, which are enriched by mesoporous silica coating (SiO2) as the signal label to simply detection solution. Lastly, to further improve the sensitivity of the sensor, the hybrid chain reaction (HCR) is introduced without the involvement of enzymes was selected, which can introduce more labeled probes by forming ultra-long double chains.

Patulin (PAT) is a mycotoxin which widely found in various fruits and their products and has toxicological effects such as fertility, carcinogenicity, and immunity, and is also a neurotoxin (Vidal et al., 2019). With the improvement of nutritional awareness, the consumption proportion of fruit products in the diet of residents is increasing worldwide. Thus, development of an ECLR-biosensor for PAT detection in possibly contaminated food to ensure its edible security has pioneering implications and value. Inspired by the above, an ultrasensitive ratiometric ECL aptasensor based on dual-potential ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in a novel ternary system for the testing of PAT was designed, which pioneered the adhibition of single luminophore ECLR-biosensor in mycotoxin analysis.

Materials and reagents. Tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) (99.95%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany), AgNO3, HAuCl4, Na2PtCl4, sodium citrate, sodium borohydride, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), and tripropylamine (TPrA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hexyl alcohol, triton x-100, cyclohexane, cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), urea, n-butanol, (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and Polydiallyl dimethyl ammonium chloride (PDDA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Patulin (PAT), Ochratoxin (OTA), Zearalenone (ZEN), Aflatoxin-B1 (AFB1), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from J&K Chemicals Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). The nucleic acid sequences used in the study were supplied from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And their sequences were listed in Table S1. All reagents were all analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water from a high-purity water system (Youpu Super Water Company, Ltd, Chengdu, China).
Apparatus is listed in Supporting information.

Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped trimetallic nanostructures (Ru@Tri) were synthesized with appropriate modifications according to the reported low-temperature method (Wang et al., 2018) (Scheme 1
A). AgNPs was synthesized first (the preparation procedure is provided in the Supporting information). In detail, 340 μL Ru(bpy)3 2+ (10 mM) and 200 mL AgNPs were dispersed in 33 mL H2O containing 2.5 g trisodium citrate. Subsequently, 2 μL HAuCl4 (2. 5 M), 2.5 μL H2PtCl6 (2 M), and 1 mL NaBH4 (0.05 M) were added quickly and stirred vigorously for 60 s in an ice bath. Finally, Ru@Tri was obtained by centrifugation and washing. And it was kept in the dark at 4 ℃. By contrast, based on the same method, AuAgPt, Ru@AuA, and Ru@PtAg were synthesized simultaneously.

The overall preparation process of SiO2-coated anth-CQDs (anth-CQDs@SiO2) was shown in Scheme 1 B. Firstly, according to the Chinese invention patent we applied for (No. CN202111359479.4), anthocyanin-derived carbon quantum dots (anth-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method without any further passivation. Briefly, anthocyanin stock solution was prepared from purple potato skins by solvent extraction. Then the obtained anthocyanin stock solution was transferred to the reaction kettle for hydrothermal reaction to prepare anth-CQDs stock solution. After filtration, dialysis and freeze-drying, purified anth-CQDs were obtained. Subsequently, amino-functionalized anth-CQDs@SiO2 were synthesized by the reverse microemulsion method to achieve the enrichment of anth-CQDs. The detailed preparation procedure is provided in the Supporting information.

Firstly, hairpin 1 (HP1) and hairpin 2 (HP2) were annealed at 90 ℃ for 5 min and cooled to room temperature naturally to form a metastable hairpin structure. Then anth-CQDs@SiO2 (0.2 mg mL−1) was mixed with 5% GA at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, HP1 and HP2 were gently stirred overnight with 100 μL GA modified anth-CQDs@SiO2 at 4 °C. After centrifugation to remove unbound HP, anth-CQDs@SiO2 labeled HP (anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP) was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and detection procedure of dual-potential ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic illustration of the dual-potential ECL aptasensor fabrication is shown in Scheme 1 C. First, the polished, cleaned glassy carbon electrode (GE, Φ = 3.0 mm) was immersed in 0.5% PDDA solution for 1 h at room temperature to obtain a positively charged GE for subsequent modification. Then 6 μL Ru@Tri solution was coated on the electrode to obtain Ru@Tri/GE via the electrostatic self-assembly method. Afterward, 5 μL complementary DNA (cDNA, 2 μM) was dropped on Ru@Tri/GE and incubated at 4 ℃ for 12 h to obtain cDNA/Ru@Tri/GE. To remove nonspecifically adsorbed cDNA, the modified electrode was treated with BSA (0.25 wt%, 5.0 μL) at room temperature for 1 h (BSA/Ru@Tri/GE). Next, 5 μL aptamer (2 μM) was introduced on the electrode surface by hybridization with cDNA at 37 ℃ for 40 min. After the formation of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA/Ru@Tri/GE), it was incubated with PAT solutions in different concentrations (PAT/Ru@Tri/GE). Subsequently, PAT/Ru@Tri/GE was incubated with a mixture solution of anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP1 and anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP2, and stirred gently at 37 ℃ for 2 h to obtain HCRcomplex/Ru@Tri/GE. At the end of each step, the modified electrode was rinsed gently with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). Finally, the HCRcomplex/Ru@Tri/GE was employed for ECL measurement.

ECL signals were recorded at potential from −1.8 V to + 1.2 V with the scanning rate of 0.1 V s−1 in PBS buffer containing 5 mM K2S2O8 (PBSECL) and PMT voltage is 600 V. PBSECL was passed passes through N2 for half an hour before use to remove dissolved O2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were operated in 5 mM and 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 solution containing 0.1 M KCl, respectively. All experiments were repeated 5 times. The cathodic ECL intensity obtained at −1.8 V was recorded as I ECL-C, and the anodic ECL intensity obtained at + 1.2 V was recorded as I ECL-A. The ratio of I ECL-A and I ECL-C (I ECL-A/I ECL-C) is related to the concentration of PAT.

PAT-free apple juice samples were added to different concentrations of PAT to obtain a final concentration of 0.5, 50, and 5 × 103 pg mL−1 through the standard addition method for recovery testing first. And six fruits and derived products containing apple, apple juice, puree, preserved fruit, fruit vinegar, and fruit wine samples in three batches were purchased from local supermarkets for reliability verification. Sample processing procedures refer to the previous report (Lu et al., 2022). 1.0 g of samples were added in 10.0 mL of ethyl acetate and mixed by vortexing for 5 min. After the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 5 min, the supernatant was concentrated to dryness by a rotary evaporator. Then the obtained power was dissolved in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and was analyzed by our proposed aptasensor first. Furthermore, referring to the national standard of the People’s Republic of China (GB5009.96–2016), all samples were validated by HPLC (detailed processing procedures are provided in the Supporting information). And each sample was tested three times in parallel.

We characterized the microscopic morphology of Ru@Tri by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). As shown in Fig. 1
A, the synthesized Ru@Tri appears as a regular cubic shape with well-defined edges, uniform dispersion, and uniform particle size. By partial enlargement view (Fig. 1 B), the average particle size of the nanocube is about 190 nm ± 10 nm. Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) was used to study the chemical composition. We can conclude from Fig. 1 C that the EDS spectrum includes characteristic peaks for Ag, Au, Pt, and Ru, indicating the presence of the four elements Au, Ag, Pt, and Ru in Ru@Tri. Moreover, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) of Ru@Tri was employed to research chemical composition and elemental valence. The XPS results further illustrate that the prepared Ru@Tri contains Au, Ag, Pt, and Ru elements. Two peaks in Ru 3p high-resolution spectra were emerge at 484.4 and 462.5 eV (Fig. 1 D), which were attributed to oxidized Ru 3p1/2 and 3p3/2, respectively (Geng et al., 2018). In the narrow spectrum of Ag (Fig. 1 E), two peaks located at 374.2 and 368.1 eV was assigned to metallic Ag 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, respectively (Yang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig, 1F, two sharp peaks with binding energies of 87.7 and 84.0 eV in the high-resolution narrow spectrum of Au 4f correspond to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, respectively. After splitting the peaks, the binding energies of 87.4 and 83.7 eV coincided with Au0, while the hills at 88.9 and 84.4 eV were attributed to Au+ (Umar et al., 2014). According to the narrow spectrum of Pt 4f (Fig. 1 G), the peaks at 74.4 and 71.0 eV were assigned to Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2. The peaks with binding energies of 73.9 and 70.7 eV were from Pt0, while the peaks with binding energies of 75.2 and 71.7 eV were attributed to Pt2+ (Fu et al., 2013). It can be concluded that the mental state is the main existing form of Ag, Au, and Pt, and Ru is doped in the initial valence state, which proves the successful preparation of Ru@Tri nanocube.

We investigated the optical and electrochemical properties of anth-CQDs first. The calculated QY is 14.5% (detailed procedures are provided in the Supporting information), which is satisfactory compared with that of the other biomass-derived CQDs without passivation (Manikandan & Lee, 2022). This may attribute to the extraction procedure. As shown in Fig. S1A, the absorption peak is located at 317 nm and blue fluorescent colors were observed under UV light irradiation at 365 nm. The maximum emission intensity at 480 nm was recorded when the excitation wavelength was 330 nm. The above can be ascribed to electronic absorption transitions such as π-π* and n-π* (Zeng et al., 2015). Moreover, in the scan range of −0.5 V to + 0.9 V, anth-CQDs modified electrode showed an obvious oxidation peak at + 0.2 V (Fig. S1B), indicating that the prepared anth-CQDs have excellent electrochemical activity and can be oxidized at + 0.2 V.

The microscopic morphology and surface functionalities of anth-CQDs and anth-CQDs@SiO2 were represented and interpreted. The anth-CQDs prepared from anthocyanin extracts exhibited a good distribution of near spherical nanospheres with an average particle size of approximately 4.6 nm in the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image (Fig. 1 H) (size distribution of anth-CQDs was provided in Fig. S2). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of anth-CQDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23° (Fig. 1 I), which corresponds to (002) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon, demonstrating anth-CQDs have graphitic structure. Considering the interlayer spacing of graphite (0.33 nm), the obtained anth-CQD possesses graphitic properties with highly disordered carbon atoms (Barati et al., 2015). After wrapping using the microemulsion method, Fig. 1 J shows that anth-CQDs@SiO2 present as a sphere with a slightly rougher profile, shallow surrounding contours, and deep core, meaning the encapsulation of the anth-CQDs in SiO2 nanospheres was achieved. To further explore the successful preparation of anth-CQDs@SiO2, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of anth-CQDs and anth-CQDs@SiO2 were recorded (Fig. 1 K). From absorption spectra of anth-CQDs (curve a), a broad absorption band at 3416 cm−1 attributed to OH group, and the peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 are detected for the carboxyl CO stretching and COC groups, respectively (Hu et al., 2021). This indicates that anth-CQDs are rich in functional groups on their surface and solubility is good. From the absorption spectra of anth-CQDs@SiO2 (curve b), in addition to the characteristic peaks of anth-CQDs, a distinct absorption peak at 1095 cm−1 attributable to the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of the Si-O-Si bond, illustrating the successful encapsulation of SiO2. Moreover, the absorption bond at 3416 cm−1 is assigned to OH or NH group and the absorption bond at 1404 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching of CN, which proves the surface is functionalized with amino groups (Chen et al., 2016).

Because anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP was introduced in large quantities to GE dependent on the HCR reaction, it is necessary to verify the formation of HCR reaction first. Agarose gel (mass fraction 3%) was used to characterize and validate HCR amplification and aptamer-triggered HCR systems (Meng et al., 2022). And Detailed experimental procedures are presented in Supporting information. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a short single-stranded cDNA exhibited the fastest migration rate (lane a). A bit slow lane b was observed, which revealed the formation of dsDNA between cDNA and aptamer. When HP1 and HP2 were mixed with cDNA, HCR reaction was executable and a slower and wider migration band generated (lane c). However, after the formation of dsDNA, no band of polymer complex was observed by adding HP1 and HP2, but only two bands of low molecular weight were formed (lane d), which were dsDNA and HP, respectively. Furthermore, this also indicates that only HP1 and HP2 would not induce self-HCR. After PAT was introduced, cDNA was exposed due to the specific binding of aptamer to PAT, and HP1 and HP2 were consumed in large quantities to form long dsDNA, resulting in the polymer complex bond like lane c (lane e). The image of agarose gel electrophoresis was consistent with the expected and reported results, indicating the feasibility of the aptasesor based on HCR reaction.

EIS and CV were employed to characterize the construction process of the ECL aptasensor with [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as redox probe (Fig. 2
B). A relevant equivalent circuit (top-left inset in Fig. 2B) was utilized to fit the EIS data. Compared with bare GE (curve a, 296 Ω), after PDDA modification, charge-transfer resistance (Rct) was reduced to 127 Ω since the good electron transfer capacity of PDDA (curve b). As the partial enlargement of curve b and curve c (bottom-right inset in Fig. 2B) shows, the Rct further decreased to 107 Ω (curve c) after Ru@Tri was modified on GE due to the elevation of effective electrode areas and superior conductivity of noble metal. When the electrode was incubated with cDNA, BSA, and aptamer in sequence, the Rct increased gradually to 615 Ω (curve d), 840 Ω (curve e), and 1184 Ω (curve f) because cDNA, BSA, and dsDNA could hinder electron transfer. While after the addition of PAT, the aptamer specifically bonded to the PAT and detached from the GE surface, resulting in the descent of Rct (curve g, 1083 Ω). Finally, by HCR amplified reaction, plenty of anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP1 and anth-CQDs@SiO2-HP2 were introduced on GE (curve h), which induced the obvious elevation of Rct (1347 Ω), owing to the obstruction of the charge transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe by HP. The above results demonstrated the successful fabrication of HCRcomplex/Ru@Tri/GE. Furthermore, the fabrication of the ECL aptasensor was also characterized by CV measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 C, compared with bare GE (curve a), the peak current increased when PDDA was modified on the bare GE (curve b). As expected, the peak current further increased after Ru@Tri was immobilized on the modified electrode (curve c). As can be seen, after successive incubation of cDNA, BSA, and aptamer with electrode, orderly decreased redox peak currents were presented, separately (curve d, e, and f). Subsequently, with the binding between PAT and aptamer that resulted in aptamer detached from the electrode surface, the peak current increased slightly (curve g). Finally, the HCR reaction caused an obvious decline in redox peak current (curve h). These CV results were well-consistent with that of EIS, indicating that the ECL aptasensor was successfully constructed.

To further verify the feasibility of this dual-potential ECL aptasensor, CV and ECL profile of the constructed dual-potential ECL aptasensor before (curve a) and after (curve b) incubated with 1 pg mL−1 PAT. As shown in Fig. 2 D, as expected, significant cathodic ECL responses and faint anodic ECL responses were generated when PAT is absent. Accordingly, a big reduction peak of S2O8 2− at −1.27 V and a weak oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at 1.2 V in the CV profile were exhibited. When the PAT is present, large amounts of anth-CQDs@SiO2 were introduced to GE, resulting in the reduction of cathodic ECL responses and a considerable increase of anodic ECL. Meanwhile, the oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was elevated while the reduction peak of S2O8 2− was diminished. The above results demonstrate that the presence of PAT can cause the expected changes in dual-potential ECL signal. Thus the concentration of PAT could be determined from the ratio changes of ECL intensity at the anode and cathode of Ru(pby)3 2+ (I ECL-A/I ECL-C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible reaction mechanism of the ratiometric ECL aptaensor
ce:section.ce:para:
According to previous studies, Ag plays a role in promoting the formation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ to Ru(bpy)3 2+*, which can enhance the ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ both at the cathode and anode (Lu et al., 2013). To verify the superiority of Ru@Tri (Ru@AuAgPt), AgNPs are always added, and a comparison was implemented by recording the ECL-potential curves of AuAgPt, Ru@Bi (Ru@AuAg and Ru@PtAg) and Ru@Tri modified GE in PBSECL. As displayed in Fig. 3
A, when Ru(bpy)3 2+ was not added to the modification material (curve a), there is almost no ECL signal at the cathode and anode, which proves that Ru(bpy)3 2+ is the only emitter in this system. While Ru@AuAg (curve b) and Ru@PtAg (curve c) as the modifier, an obvious cathodic ECL signal was observed. Remarkably, the strongest ECL was obtained from Ru@AuAgPt modified GE. The above results may be attributed to the electrocatalytic effect of Au, Ag, and Pt on K2S2O8, promoting the conversion of S2O8 2− to SO4 •− (Lei et al., 2018). Moreover, due to the synergistic interaction between the alloy atoms and the high refractive index surface, the three metal alloys show better electrocatalytic performance (Zhou et al., 2020). Thus, Ru@Tri enables high-intensity cathode ECL at low K2S2O8 concentrations. Furthermore, the inset in Fig. 3A reflects the anodic ECL response of the modifier in PBSECL. It was observed that a weak ECL signal was generated at the anode when there was no anodic coreactant in the detection substrate since the annihilation mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+. And noble metal nanoparticles can prevent Ru(bpy)3 2+ aggregation and self-quenching. Therefore, Ru@Tri is a excellent modifying material, acting as the Ru(bpy)3 2+ carrier and CCA.

UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra were employed to investigate the anodic ECL mechanism between anth-CQDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Fig. 3 B and the insert). The absorption of anth-CQDs is at 317 nm (curve a). A sharp and a broad absorption peak at 286 nm and at 454 nm of Ru(bpy)3 2+ are emerged, simultaneously (curve b). Similarly, in the insert, anth-CQDs (curve a) and Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve c) showed distinct FL emissions at 480 nm and 620 nm, respectively. In anth-CQDs/Ru(bpy)3 2+ mixture, both anth-CQDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ keep their UV–vis absorption and FL properties (curve c), illustrating that anth-CQDs in the ground-state or excited-state is not going to react with Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Liu at el., 2020). To further verify the anodic ECL mechanism, CV curves of Ru(bpy)3 2+, anth-CQDs, and their mixture were recorded at bare GE in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4). As shown in Fig. 3 C, Ru(bpy)3 2+ exhibits reversible redox peaks at + 1.2 V and −0.5 V, respectively (curve a), which are derived from the oxidation of Ru(bpy)3 3+ to Ru(bpy)3 2+ at + 1.2 V and the reduction to Ru(bpy)+ at −0.5 V. In curve b, an oxidation peak of anth-CQDs appeared at + 0.2 V, indicating that anth-CQDs can be oxidized at + 0.2 V and generated anth-CQDs·+. And when they were coexisting, the oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ increased while the reduction peak reduced, indicating the reaction between the oxidation products of anth-CQDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+. The above proves that anth-CQDs can reduce the electrogenerated Ru(bpy)3 3+ by generating a reductive intermediate, resulting in excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+* and the anodic ECL. And there may be a similar mechanism in Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPrA system.

On this basis, the effects of anth-CQD, anth-CQD@SiO2, and commercial TPrA on Ru@Tri were discussed. As shown in Fig. 3 D, Ru@Tri/GE displayed strong cathodic ECL and weak anodic ECL in PBSECL (curve a). After 5 μL anth-CQD (0.2 mg mL−1) was dropped on Ru@Tri/GE, the anodic ECL was notably enhanced and cathodic ECL was reduced. This may owe to direct and rapid reaction between Ru@Tri and anth-CQD on the electrode surface. Thus, the amount of Ru@Tri reacting with K2S2O8 in PBS descended accordingly. And when 5 μL anth-CQD@SiO2 (0.2 mg mL−1) was dropped on Ru@Tri/GE, this trend was further reinforced (curve c). This illustrated that enriched anth-CQDs could produce more obvious effects on Ru@Tri. Furthermore, a similar anode ECL as curve c was produced by Ru@Tri/GE in PBSECL containing 5 mM TPrA. This implies that anth-CQDs are the potential alternatives to TPrA. Noteworthily, the coreactant introduced into the electrode surface can improve the ECL efficiency and simplify the detection environment simultaneously.

Dissolved O2 may affect the ECL, especially in dual-potential ECL systems. In Fig. 3 E, the minimal I ECL-A/I ECL-C was obtained from Ru@Tri/GE in O2-saturated PBSECL containing 0.2 mg mL−1 anth-CQDs@SiO2. While in the N2 saturated testing atmosphere, I ECL-A/I ECL-C of Ru@Tri/GE is the maximum. Compared to an untreated aqueous solution (20% O2), anodic and cathodic ECL of Ru/GE was visibly inhibited in O2 saturated aqueous solution (100% O2). In N2-saturated PBSECL (O2 free) in the presence of 0.2 mg mL−1 anth-CQDs@SiO2, the anodic ECL increased slightly while the cathodic ECL decreased slightly. This phenomenon may be attributed to dissolved O2 is a nonnegligible quencher for Ru(bpy)3 2+ anode ECL through energy transfer. However, for the cathode, a high concentration of O2 may quench ECL while a low concentration of O2 may enhance ECL as an electron transfer medium (Dai et al., 2015).

Concerning previous reports, the possible ECL mechanisms of this study are explained in Fig. 3 F. When Ru@Tri is immobilized on GE, at the cathode, Ru(bpy)3 2+ and S2O8 2− gain electrons to form Ru(bpy)3 + and SO4 ·−, respectively. Then a reaction between them occurs to form excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+* (blue line) and generate a cathodic ECL signal. Moreover, Tri plays electrocatalysis activity on S2O8 2−, hasting generation of SO4 ·− (red line), leading to a stronger ECL emit. On the other hand, at the anode, Ru(bpy)3 2+ loses an electron to form Ru(bpy)3 3+, and Ru(bpy)3 3+ and anth-CQDs undergo redox reactions to form anth-CQDs·+. Further, anth-CQDs·+ react with Ru(bpy)3 3+ to form Ru(bpy)3 2+*, and an obvious anodic ECL signal was observed.

In this ternary ECL system, Ru@Tri plays dual roles of luminophore and CCA, anth-CQDs@SiO2 is the anodic coreactant, K2S2O8 is the cathodic coreactant, and HCR is a signal amplification strategy. To achieve the sensitive analysis of PAT, the concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ during the preparation of Ru@Tri, the concentration of anth-CQDs@SiO2 used to label HP, the concentration of K2S2O8 and pH of PBSECL, and the incubation time of HCR was optimized. The corresponding results and explanation were exhibited in the Supporting information. In the preparation process of Ru@Tri, 10 mM is chosen as the optimal addition concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+. 0.2 mg mL−1 is the optimal concentration of anth-CQDs@SiO2. 5 mM is the selective concentration of K2S2O8 in PBSECL. And pH 7.4 is believed as the optimal pH of PBSECL. The maximal I ECL-A/I ECL-C was obtained when the incubation time of HCR is 2 h.

The sensitivity of the ratiometric ECL aptasensor was investigated by recording anodic and cathodic ECL responses after incubation with PAT. As shown in Fig. 4
A, with an incremental concentration of PAT, the I ECL-A gradually increased and the I ECL-C gradually decreased. And the I ECL-A/I ECL-C showed a linear relation with the logarithm of PAT concentration ranging from 0.1 pg mL−1 to 10 ng mL−1. The linear calibration equation is I ECL-A/I ECL-C = 1.925 + 0.464 lg (c PAT/ng mL−1) with a correction coefficient of r = 0.9981 (Fig. 4 B). And the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.05 pg mL−1 (LOD = 3σb/slope, the σb on behalf of the standard deviation of eleven blanks). Then, analytical performances of the proposed aptasensor and recently reported biosensors (containing single-signal and ratiometric double-signal biosensors) for PAT detection were summarized in Table S2. As can be seen, a comparable or even lower LOD and a satisfactory linear range were gained via the proposed sensing platform, demonstrating that the ratiometric dual-signal ECL sensing strategy can effectively improve the detection performance of the biosensors.

The reproducibility calculated based on anodic and cathodic single ECL response and their ratio were investigated by establishing aptasensors incubated with 0.1 ng mL−1 PAT for seven consecutive days. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of I ECL-C and I ECL-A is 4.3% and 5.7% respectively. While RSD of I ECL-A/I ECL-C is 2.9% (Fig. 4 D). It shows that the ratio quantitative mode improves the precision of the ECL aptasensor. Moreover, continuous cyclic scans and long stability of the aptasensor with 1 pg mL−1 PAT were discussed. In Fig. 4 E, under 7 cycles of successive potential scans (blank curve), there are no obvious fluctuations of dual-potential ECL response were observed and RSD was lower than 2.7%. Simultaneously, the constructed aptasensor was stored at 4 °C and ECL detection was performed every 5 days. Until the 28th day, I ECL-A/I ECL-C is down to 90% of the initial value. Above indicates that the aptasensor is a stable sensing platform.

Additionally, in order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the proposed aptasensor, ECL response obtained from potential interfering mycotoxins including Fumonisin (FB), Ochratoxin A (OTA), Zearalenone (ZEN) were investigated. And their effects on the value of I ECL-A/I ECL-C were recorded and shown in Fig. 4 F. In contrast to the blank, the negligible changes of I ECL-A/I ECL-C were generated after incubated with 10-fold interferents respectively, including 1 ng mL−1 AFB1, OTA, and ZEN. While for 0.1 ng mL−1 PAT at lower concentrations or their mixture (0.1 ng mL−1 PAT blended with 1 ng mL−1 AFB1, OTA ZEN), a stronger I ECL-A/I ECL-C was obtained. These results demonstrate the high selectivity of the proposed aptasensor.

The recovery experiment was adopted to verify the reliability and accuracy of our proposed ECL aptasensor. Satisfactory recoveries were shown in Table S3, which are in the range of 96% −106%. And calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) is equal to or lower than 5.1%. Thus, these results indicate the high reliability and accuracy of the constructed ECL aptasensor.

In addition, to further evaluate the effectiveness and practicability, 18 samples of fruit and their products were analyzed by the proposed aptasensor and validated in parallel by HPLC. As shown in Table 1 , two preserved fruit samples and one fruit vinegar sample were tested positive for PAT, but are far below national limitation standards (GB 2761–2017). Moreover, the obtained results of the two methods were in good agreement, and the differences were not statistically significant after analysis by student t-test (P > 0.05). This gives us some rethink that fruit products that have been stored, processed, and transported for a long time may have a greater chance of being contaminated by PAT. Moreover, compared to the previously reported PAT biosensors, the proposed ECL aptasensor has been applied to the detection of PAT in various kinds of fruit products (Table S2), implying its practical application potential.

In this study, an ultrasensitive and stable ratiometric ECL aptasensor based on a novel ternary ECL system was first proposed for PAT determination. In this ternary ECL system, Ru@Tri prepared by a simple low-temperature method innovatively integrated the luminophore (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and CCA (trimetal). With the help of synergistic catalytic effect to S2O8 2− by trimetal, which proved is superior to single metal or bimetal, excellent cathodic ECL characteristics of Ru@Tri were demonstrated. Moreover, anth-CQDs derived from purple potato peel were first employed as a green anodic coreactant. Anth-CQDs@SiO2 showed significant anodic ECL amplification properties to Ru@Tri. Therefore, anth-CQDs could be considered as a substitute for highly toxic TPrA. HCR was adopted as an enzyme-free amplification method have further achieved signal amplification without limiting reaction conditions. Given these advantages, the constructed ratiometric ECL aptasensor for PAT detection exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, reproducibility, stability, and satisfactory recovery. When applied to a variety of fruits and products, the result obtained from our proposed assay is in agreement with those provided by the HPLC. Furthermore, this is also a universal strategy of ECLR-sensor for the detection of mycotoxin in real samples, which holds great significance to ensure food security.

Liangrui Lv: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. QingQing Chen: Formal analysis, Software, Validation. Chunyang Jing: Methodology, Validation. Xiaoying Wang: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX21_0163).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135780.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N, S co-doped carbon dots with orange luminescence synthesized through polymerization and carbonization reaction of amino acids
Applied Surface Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zeng, Y.W., Ma, D.K., Wang, W., Chen, J.J., Zhou, L., Zheng, Y.Z., Yu, K., & Huang, S.M. (2015). N, S co-doped carbon dots with orange luminescence synthesized through polymerization and carbonization reaction of amino acids. Applied Surface Science, 342, 136–143.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive aptasensing of insulin based on hollow porous C3N4/S2O8 2−/AuPtAg ECL ternary system and DNA walker amplification
Biosensors and Bioelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, X.M., Zhang, W., Wang, Z., Han, J., Xie, G., & Chen, S.P. (2020). Ultrasensitive aptasensing of insulin based on hollow porous C3N4/S2O82−/AuPtAg ECL ternary system and DNA walker amplification. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 148, 111795.
